context: TypeScript codebase. src/utils contains 14 files: formatDate.ts (one-liner wrapping date-fns.format), capitalize.ts (str[0].toUpperCase()+str.slice(1)), clamp.ts (Math.min/Math.max), 8 more one-liners, plus three files that each only re-export from another package. There are no domain modules in src/utils — every file is a pass-through to a library or a string/number operator. Tests for src/utils import the lib directly and ignore the wrappers.
expected_behavior: Phase 1: applies the deletion test to each pass-through file and confirms that deleting any of them moves complexity to ONE call site (or zero), not to N
Phase 2: produces FEW or ZERO 'deepen' candidates here — the right output is mostly 'eliminate / inline' candidates, NOT new interfaces
Names the anti-pattern explicitly: pass-through modules without behavior to hide are not deepening candidates, they are deletion candidates
If any candidate is presented, it is structured as 'eliminate <file>, inline into <caller>' with the deletion-test justification, not as 'design a new interface for <file>'
Does not invent a domain to wrap these utilities in (no 'TextFormattingModule' or 'NumberUtils' speculative module)
Closes Phase 2 by noting that src/utils is structurally healthy at the file level — the friction (if any) is breadth, and breadth alone is not a deepening problem
failure_modes: Fabricates 8 deepening candidates that each wrap one utility behind a 'service'-style interface
Promotes pass-through wrappers to 'modules' that 'need a deeper interface' — exactly the inverse of the deletion test
Generates 'TextUtils', 'NumberUtils', 'DateUtils' grouping modules where none of the utilities share invariants or ordering — speculative grouping
Skips the deletion test entirely and lists every file as a candidate
